text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, reduces the hernia contents, and closes the fascial defect in a patient with an incisional hernia.

The surgeon reduces abdominal pressure to six to eight millimeters of mercury before mesh fixation. The fascial defect is closed along its long axis using a sero-barred slowly absorbable suture. The suture is run continuously along the defect's length, tightened at the opposite end, then run back in the opposite direction to create overlapping, and tightened again.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions and reduces the hernia contents in a patient with a recurrent incisional hernia. The mesh is left in place due to dense adhesion to the soft tissue, and the fascial defect is closed.
The surgeon reduces the abdominal pressure to six to eight millimeters of mercury and closes the hernia defect along its long axis using a zero-barred, slowly absorbable suture.
The surgeon tightens the suture along the length of a large fascial defect. A second suture is then applied in the opposite direction, from caudal to cranial, to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon secures the mesh circumferentially over the closed fascial defect using a tool-barred, slowly absorbable suture.
